text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic cholecystectomy by entering the abdomen via an open technique at the umbilicus, establishing standard port positions. An anterior peritoneal incision is made and extended cranially, followed by dissection of fibrofatty tissue to delineate Calot's triangle. Using a monopolar hook electrode with coagulating current, the surgeon performs meticulous fiber-by-fiber dissection to maintain hemostasis. The transparency of the peritoneal reflection is visible as the incision is extended superiorly over the posterior peritoneum, creating a peritoneal window.

The surgeon exposes the Calot's triangle, carefully dissecting the fibrofatty tissue to achieve the critical view of safety. The cystic artery and duct are identified, and the Maryland Dissector is used to skeletonize the cystic duct. The peritoneum is stripped from the cystic duct to facilitate clip placement, ensuring the cystic duct is secured. The surrounding fatty tissue is dissected off, revealing the lower one-third of the cystic plate.

The surgeon uses a smooth grasper to dissect the final attachments to the cystic duct. An advanced bipolar device is employed to coagulate the cystic artery, followed by cutting the artery using the bipolar cutting function. The surgeon then milks the cystic duct to check for stones and applies two clips on the cystic duct side and one clip on the specimen side.
The surgeon applies a clip to the specimen side of the cystic duct, ensuring a gap between the clips. Using curved scissors, the surgeon cuts the clipped cystic duct.

The surgeon uses a bipolar energy device to cut fibrous tissue that could bleed, then switches to a monopolar hook to make lateral peritoneal incisions. Proper traction is applied to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed, addressing dense fibrous attachments with the hook. The left hand instrument is repositioned closer to the dissecting area near the infundibulum.

The surgeon inspects the cystic duct stem, confirming no bleeding is present. The final fibrous attachments of the gallbladder are dissected, ensuring complete hemostasis. The gallbladder is then removed intact through the epigastric port after suctioning and removing bile.
